title: Long range proximity effects in planar structures involving the halfmetal ferromagnet La0.7Sr0.3MnO3 and Pt interlayers
authors: Junxiang Yao, Julian van Doorn, Mariona Cabero, Jan Aarts
abstract: Over the last decade, there has been steady research on superconducting junctions with a ferromagnet as the weak link, and where triplet correlations can transport supercurrents over a substantial distances. Of particular interest are halfmetallic ferromagnets, in which only one spin band is present, so that, presumably, the induced supercurrent is fully spin-polarized. We have earlier reported on a study of triplet transport in planar La0.7Sr0.3MnO3(LSMO) nanostrip Josephson junctions with NbTi superconducting contacts, where we found high values for the supercurrents, and large junction lengths (up to 1.3 μm). Here, we extend that work by studying the dependence of the critical current Ic on the length of the nanostrip between the contacts and the width of the strip. All junctions show strong supercurrents, but we do not observe simple systematics. Apparently, the fabrication process does not allow sufficient control over some of its parameters. To gain more insight in the mechanism for triplet generation at the LSMO/NbTi interface, we also studied the effect of Pt as an interlayer between the LSMO and the NbTi. For this, we etched a NbTi/Pt electrode structure on a full film of LSMO. The results are highly promising, showing sharp superconducting transitions and zero-resistance states being reached at an electrode distance of 2 μm, with indications that larger distances should be feasible.
summary: The researchers investigated long-range proximity effects in junctions using the half-metal LSMO. They found that adding a Pt interlayer maintains strong supercurrents, suggesting that the heavy metal promotes triplet generation through spin-orbit coupling at the interface.
main_problem: Investigating the mechanism of long-range triplet supercurrent transport and the role of the interface in LSMO-based Josephson junctions.
main_result: The study demonstrates that triplet supercurrents persist even with a Pt interlayer, suggesting that the Pt layer promotes triplet generation, potentially via Rashba-like spin-orbit coupling.
method: Transport measurements (R vs T, I-V, SQI) and advanced microscopy (STEM-EELS, HAADF) were used to analyze junction properties and interface chemistry.
model_or_system: Planar Josephson junctions and full-film electrode structures consisting of a half-metallic ferromagnet (La0.7Sr0.3MnO3), a Pt interlayer, and superconducting NbTi contacts.
key_observables: Critical current density (Jc), resistance (R), superconducting transition temperature (Tc), and Superconducting Quantum Interference (SQI) patterns.
important_parameters: Junction length (L), junction width (w), Pt interlayer thickness (7 nm), and temperature (down to 2 K).
main_assumptions: The Pt interlayer is assumed to be wetting and non-oxidizing.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows device layouts; Fig 2 displays transport properties (R vs T, I-V, Jc vs T) for long junctions; Fig 3 shows resistance for full-film devices; Fig 4 shows SQI patterns; Fig 6 shows high-resolution STEM-EELS imaging and atomic profiles.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of triplet transport, describes the fabrication and characterization of long-junction and full-film devices, analyzes the impact of Pt interlayers on supercurrents, and concludes with structural/chemical analysis of the interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Layout and SEM image of the LJ (upper row) and FF (lower row) devices. (a) NbTi top layer contacts (blue) on LSMO/LSAT, with an LSMO connecting bridge (yellow). (b) SEM picture of device LJ12 (L = 1.58 µm), with the LSMO bridge in false-color purple. The scale bar is 5 µm. (c) Top contacts on a NbTi (blue)/ Pt (white) double layer on a full film of LSMO (yellow) on LSAT. (d) SEM picture of device FF11 (L = 2.1 µm). The scale bar is 10 µm.

Figure 2: Transport properties of the LJ devices. (a) Resistance R versus temperature T for the devices as indicated. (b) Current I versus voltage V for LJ12 (L = 1.58 µm) at 5.9 K, 5.7 K and 5.5 K. (c) Critical current Jc as function of temperature T for the LJ devices as indicated, with the highest temperature showing the lowest Jc. The starred devices were reported on before [7]. The inset shows the behavior of Jc at 2 K as funciont of L, color-coded in the same manner as the main plot.

Figure 3: Resistance R versus temperature T for the three full film devices (a) FF7, (b) FF9 and (c) FF11, discussed in the text. In (a) two traces are shown, one for increasing temperature (orange circles), and one for decreasing temperature (blue triangles). In (c) the change in R(T) after another 2 minute Ar-etch step is also shown. The measurements were performed with an applied current of 1 µA.

Figure 4: Superconducting Quantum Interference (SQI) patterns, represented as a color plot of voltage V versus applied current I, for (a) LJ12, measured with an OOP field (z-direction) at 5.65 K, (b) FF11 with an IP field along y (perpendicular to the axis between the contacts), at 4.65 K, and (c) FF11 with an OOP field, at 4.65 K.

Figure 5: AFM images of the surface of (a) an LSMO/Ag bilayer and (b) an LSMO/Pt bilayer. In the case of Ag, high roughness and island formation is visible. In the case of Pt, the roughness is no more than 2 nm.
